ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated papadum code; A0CKP (flatbreads and crackers) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for papadum; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: may contain traces of wheat depending on manufacturer

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of plain papad consists of 4 pieces, weighing approximately 40 g in total, typically served as an appetiser or accompaniment to an Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Papad, fried — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), entry for fried papad (urad dal base). Plain papad is the standard urad-dal-based variety; values represent the fried form as commonly served in restaurants.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Lentil-based products and legume crackers
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for plain papad exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is estimated by analogy with urad dal (black gram) preparations and lentil-based crackers reported in Atkinson et al. (2008), which list lentil-based products in the GI range 26–52. A midpoint estimate of 48 is used, consistent with the high protein and fibre content of urad dal flour which attenuates glycaemic response. gi_category is 'low' (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per serving = (48 × 22.8 g carbohydrate per 40 g serving) / 100 = 10.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~1050 mg per 100 g; ~420 mg per 4-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit plain papad consumption to one or two pieces per occasion, as the high salt content (~420 mg sodium per standard 4-piece serving) contributes meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load (~10.9 per 4-piece serving) with low GI (48) due to high protein and fibre from urad dal
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain papad has a low glycaemic index (estimated 48) owing to its urad dal base, but the moderate glycaemic load per serving means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it in controlled portions and avoid deep-fried preparations in favour of roasted versions to limit total carbohydrate and fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content; cholesterol-free with beneficial plant protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While plain papad is cholesterol-free and provides plant-based protein from urad dal, its high sodium content and fat from frying present a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer roasted papad and restrict portion size to manage sodium and total fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain papad (also called papadum, poppadom, or appalam in South India) is a thin, crispy disc-shaped cracker made primarily from black gram flour (urad dal), salt, and spices such as black pepper and cumin. It is a staple accompaniment in Indian cuisine, served before or alongside meals as an appetiser or snack. Papad can be prepared by deep-frying, roasting over an open flame, or microwaving; the fried version is most common in restaurant settings and delivers a distinctly light, airy crunch. Nutritionally, plain papad is notable for its high protein content (~22.5 g per 100 g) derived from urad dal, significant dietary fibre (~5.8 g per 100 g), and a low glycaemic index (estimated 48), making it a relatively favourable snack for blood sugar management in moderate portions. However, its sodium content is high (~1050 mg per 100 g), which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, plain papad is widely served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a starter, often accompanied by chutneys. A standard restaurant serving is 4 pieces (~40 g), providing approximately 180 kcal, 9 g protein, 5.6 g fat, and 22.8 g carbohydrate.
common_user_queries: How many calories in plain papad?
How many pieces of papad in a serving?
Is roasted papad healthier than fried papad?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), which is the primary approved source for Indian dishes and provides an exact match for fried papad. GI is an analogous estimate based on urad dal lentil products from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) as no direct papad GI measurement is available; this reduces confidence from high to medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the fried papad form as this is the standard restaurant preparation. Roasted papad would have lower fat (~2–4 g per 100 g) and energy (~350 kcal per 100 g). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN for this entry. 3. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN for this entry. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for traditional urad dal papad with no hydrogenated fat). 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this entry. 6. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this entry. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this entry. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this entry. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this entry. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this entry. 12. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — entirely plant-based product). 13. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; negligible in processed legume crackers). 14. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement study on papad would be required to upgrade confidence to high. 15. The input record listed food_group as 'Mixed'; reclassified to 'Snacks' as papad is a cracker/snack product. 16. Serving size of 40 g (4 pieces) is based on standard restaurant portion; individual piece weight approximately 10 g each.
